fda_value_type: No current FDA commodity-specific lead action level
fda_basis: No current formula-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes infant formula
eu_basis: as placed on market as powder
prop65_basis: Illustrative 100 g/day powder-intake screen; formula-specific exposure model required
ppb_interpretation: All values are shown in ppb, but the FDA entry is a not-established status and the Prop 65 value is an exposure conversion, not a commodity limit.
hmtc_use: Do not borrow FDA processed-baby-food action levels for formula; use basis-matched occurrence data and the EU powder ceiling as external legal context.
public_note: No U.S. FDA formula-specific lead action level is currently established; the EU powder ceiling is a legal backstop, not a clean-product target.

fda_value_type: No current FDA commodity-specific lead action level
fda_basis: No current formula-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes infant formula
eu_basis: as placed on market as powder
prop65_basis: Illustrative 100 g/day powder-intake screen; formula-specific exposure model required
ppb_interpretation: All values are shown in ppb, but the FDA entry is a not-established status and the Prop 65 value is an exposure conversion, not a commodity limit.
hmtc_use: Do not borrow FDA processed-baby-food action levels for formula; use basis-matched occurrence data and the EU powder ceiling as external legal context.
public_note: No U.S. FDA formula-specific lead action level is currently established; soy formula needs its own occurrence review because soy/mineral inputs can shift the metal profile.

fda_value_type: No current FDA commodity-specific lead action level
fda_basis: No current formula-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes infant formula
eu_basis: as placed on market as liquid
prop65_basis: Illustrative 800 g/day ready-to-feed intake screen; formula-specific exposure model required
ppb_interpretation: All values are shown in ppb, but the FDA entry is a not-established status and the Prop 65 value is an exposure conversion, not a commodity limit.
hmtc_use: Do not compare RTF formula to dry-powder limits; use prepared/liquid occurrence data and the EU liquid ceiling as external legal context.
public_note: RTF formula usually has low ppb concentrations because it is already diluted, so serving-based exposure screens can be much lower than legal ceilings.

fda_value_type: No current FDA commodity-specific lead action level
fda_basis: No current formula-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes infant formula
eu_basis: as placed on market as liquid
prop65_basis: Illustrative 800 g/day ready-to-feed intake screen; formula-specific exposure model required
ppb_interpretation: All values are shown in ppb, but the FDA entry is a not-established status and the Prop 65 value is an exposure conversion, not a commodity limit.
hmtc_use: Do not compare RTF formula to dry-powder limits; use prepared/liquid occurrence data and the EU liquid ceiling as external legal context.
public_note: RTF soy formula needs basis-matched occurrence review; the Prop 65 ppb screen is driven by assumed daily intake volume.

row_label: Baby cereals and grain products, dry (non-rice)
fda_scope: Dry infant cereals for children under 2; FDA does not split the lead action level by rice versus non-rice
eu_basis: processed cereal-based food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 dry instant infant cereal RACC of 15 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA and EU both map to 20 ppb for lead; the Prop 65 value is a one-serving exposure conversion and would fall with higher daily intake.
hmtc_use: Use 20 ppb as an external regulatory cap/context; occurrence percentiles decide whether HMTc can justify a lower standard.
public_note: A 20 ppb cereal can be legally aligned while still not representing best-in-class occurrence if the category median is lower.

row_label: Baby cereals and grain products, dry (rice-based)
fda_scope: Dry infant cereals for children under 2; lead action level is not split by rice status
eu_basis: processed cereal-based food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 dry instant infant cereal RACC of 15 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA and EU both map to 20 ppb for lead; rice status mainly changes arsenic concern and occurrence profile, not the lead ceiling.
hmtc_use: Use 20 ppb as the lead regulatory cap/context, while treating rice as an ingredient-driver signal for iAs, Cd, and Pb occurrence.
public_note: Rice-based cereal can sit near legal lead limits and still be a high-priority HMTc category because rice changes the broader contaminant profile.

fda_scope: Fruits for babies and young children under 2
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA is 10 ppb and EU is 20 ppb, while the Prop 65 serving-equivalent screen is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use FDA 10 ppb as a regulatory cap/context, EU 20 ppb as a looser legal ceiling, and occurrence medians/P90s to set any HMTc target.
public_note: A puree can be below FDA and EU values while exceeding a Prop 65 serving-based screen.

fda_scope: Vegetables excluding single-ingredient root vegetables for babies and young children under 2
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA is 10 ppb and EU is 20 ppb, while the Prop 65 serving-equivalent screen is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use FDA 10 ppb as a regulatory cap/context and occurrence evidence to determine whether lower HMTc targets are feasible.
public_note: Non-root vegetable products should not inherit the 20 ppb root-vegetable FDA value unless the product is a covered single-ingredient root vegetable.

fda_value_type: FDA final guidance action level for covered single-ingredient root vegetables
fda_basis: single-ingredient carrot or sweet potato baby food, as sold
fda_scope: Single-ingredient carrots or sweet potatoes; root mixtures can route to the 10 ppb mixture value
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g
ppb_interpretation: The FDA 20 ppb value applies only to covered single-ingredient root vegetables; the Prop 65 screen is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use the FDA root-vegetable cap only when the product scope matches; compare root occurrence separately because soil uptake drives the upper tail.
public_note: Root purees can be under the FDA root value and still be high relative to fruit or non-root vegetable occurrence.

fda_scope: Single-ingredient meats and meat-based mixtures for babies and young children under 2
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA is 10 ppb and EU is 20 ppb, while the Prop 65 serving-equivalent screen is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use FDA 10 ppb as regulatory cap/context, then rely on meat/poultry occurrence evidence and ingredient drivers for any tighter HMTc threshold.
public_note: Regulatory alignment alone does not show whether a meat puree is low relative to measured category occurrence.

fda_value_type: FDA final guidance action level when covered as a mixture
fda_scope: No separate single-ingredient fish lead value in FDA 2025 baby-food guidance; fish-containing mixtures can map to the mixture value
eu_basis: baby food or infant/young-child mixed meal as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g
ppb_interpretation: The FDA 10 ppb value is a scope-dependent mixture mapping; the Prop 65 serving-equivalent screen is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use as external context only until product-scope review confirms whether the specific fish product is a covered mixture.
public_note: Fish-containing foods need separate mercury/speciation treatment; the lead ppb row should not be treated as the whole risk story.

fda_scope: Mixtures including grain- and meat-based mixtures for babies and young children under 2
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g; toddler dinner RACC is 170 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA is 10 ppb and EU is 20 ppb; the Prop 65 equivalent is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day or 2.9 ppb at 170 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use FDA 10 ppb as regulatory cap/context and keep rice status separate so non-rice occurrence is not silently pooled with rice-containing meals.
public_note: Mixed meals often have ingredient-driven variance; the ppb table helps distinguish legal compliance from actual category position.

fda_scope: Mixtures including grain-based mixtures; rice-containing status does not make the dry-infant-cereal lead action level apply
eu_basis: baby food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 strained/junior ready-to-serve infant food RACC of 110 g; toddler dinner RACC is 170 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA is 10 ppb and EU is 20 ppb; the Prop 65 equivalent is about 4.5 ppb at 110 g/day or 2.9 ppb at 170 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use FDA 10 ppb as lead cap/context, but preserve rice as an ingredient-driver signal for arsenic and cadmium as well as lead occurrence.
public_note: Rice-containing mixed meals need their own occurrence profile; do not substitute the dry cereal lead row.

fda_value_type: FDA current Juice HACCP guidance hazard-control level
fda_basis: ready-to-drink fruit juices and fruit nectars
fda_scope: Current FDA guidance context; 2022 draft lead-in-juice guidance is not final and not for implementation
eu_value_type: EU maximum level if labelled for infants/young children
eu_basis: infant/young-child drink ready-to-use basis; ordinary juices have different EU lead levels
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 infant/young-child juice RACC of 120 mL, approximated as 120 g
ppb_interpretation: FDA current context is 50 ppb; EU infant-labelled juice is 20 ppb; Prop 65 is about 4.2 ppb at 120 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use 50 ppb as current FDA context, keep the 10/20 ppb FDA juice values as draft-only context, and split apple/non-apple before standards use.
public_note: Juice is the clearest example where a current federal context value can look loose relative to Prop 65 and modern occurrence data.

fda_value_type: No current FDA baby-food lead action level
fda_basis: No snack-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes snack foods such as puffs, rusks, and teething biscuits
eu_value_type: EU maximum level if classified as processed cereal-based infant/young-child food
eu_basis: processed cereal-based food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 infant teething/snack dry grain product RACC of 7 g
ppb_interpretation: The FDA entry is a not-established status; EU can be 20 ppb if the product is in the processed-cereal infant-food scope; Prop 65 is 71.4 ppb at 7 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use occurrence evidence only until product-scope review confirms applicable regulation; do not borrow dry infant cereal action levels for snacks.
public_note: Small serving size makes the one-serving Prop 65 ppb equivalent high, but frequent consumption or larger servings lower the equivalent.

fda_value_type: No current FDA baby-food lead action level
fda_basis: No snack-specific FDA lead action level
fda_scope: FDA 2025 processed-baby-food lead guidance excludes snack foods; infant rice cereal lead/iAs values do not automatically apply to snacks
eu_value_type: EU maximum level if classified as processed cereal-based infant/young-child food
eu_basis: processed cereal-based food as placed on market
prop65_basis: 21 CFR 101.12 infant teething/snack dry grain product RACC of 7 g
ppb_interpretation: The FDA entry is a not-established status; EU can be 20 ppb if the product is in the processed-cereal infant-food scope; Prop 65 is 71.4 ppb at 7 g/day.
hmtc_use: Use rice as a contamination-platform flag, but do not silently attach infant-rice-cereal regulatory values to rice snacks.
public_note: Rice-based snacks remain a priority because rice can drive arsenic and cadmium even where the FDA lead action level is not established.
